blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Anesthesia in the Neonate?
Neonatologist ไม่ได้วางยา — แต่ต้องรู้ว่ายาวางสลบ ทำอะไรกับทารกของเรา. (1) Anand et al Lancet 1987: ทารกที่ไม่ได้รับ adequate anesthesia → stress response ↑ hormones (epinephrine, norepinephrine, cortisol, glucagon) → ↑ complications + ↑ mortality — fentanyl เพิ่มเติมหยุดได้; (2) Neonates CAN feel pain: มี consciousness, sense of self, และ implicit memory — early pain → exaggerated responses ต่อ painful stimuli later in life + impaired neurodevelopment; (3) FDA 2016 warning: repeated/lengthy GA ใน children <3 yr อาจกระทบ brain development — แต่ GAS/PANDA/MASK trials ให้ reassurance ว่า single brief anesthetic ปลอดภัย; (4) เราต้องรู้ pharmacokinetics ของ fentanyl/morphine/propofol ใน preterm — immaturity → ↓ clearance → prolonged effect → postoperative apnea risk ใน <55–60 wk PCA; (5) PPHN, BPD, fetal Hgb, hypothermia — ทุกอย่างเปลี่ยน anesthetic response. เราคือ team ที่รับทารกกลับ NICU หลัง OR — ต้องรู้ว่ายาอะไรถูกให้ ผลข้างเคียงคืออะไร และต้องดูอะไรก่อน.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย physiologic basis ของ heightened perioperative risk ในทารก: FRC, metabolic rate, cardiac physiology, fetal Hgb, renal immaturity, thermoregulation
ระบุ risk factors สำหรับ perioperative mortality ใน neonates (weight <5 kg, ASA ≥III, sepsis, inotropic support, ventilator dependence)
อธิบาย preoperative evaluation ที่ครบถ้วน: NPO guidelines, cardiorespiratory status, lab criteria (Hgb >10 g/dL), congenital syndrome review
อธิบาย pharmacokinetics ของ inhalational agents (sevoflurane/isoflurane) ใน premature vs term neonate: MAC ตาม PCA, uptake rate, overdose risk
เปรียบเทียบ IV agents: propofol, ketamine, midazolam, fentanyl, morphine, remifentanil, dexmedetomidine — dose, use, risks ใน neonate
อธิบาย neuromuscular blocking agents: succinylcholine (ED95, contraindications), vecuronium/cisatracurium/rocuronium, reversal (neostigmine, sugammadex)
วิเคราะห์ PPHN risk perioperatively: triggers, monitoring (pre/postductal SpO2), prevention strategies
ระบุ postoperative apnea risk ตาม gestational age + PCA: Cote meta-analysis — monitoring duration หลัง surgery
อธิบาย regional anesthesia ใน neonate: caudal/spinal/epidural, fascial plane blocks, LAST risk, local anesthetic choices
เชื่อม anesthetic events กับ NICU post-op management: fentanyl residual effect, hypothermia, glucose, fluid balance, BPD exacerbation
1. Safety and Challenges of Neonatal Anesthesia
1.1 Perioperative Risk Profile
Perioperative surgical risk ↑ inversely with age — neonates = most vulnerable surgical patients; มากกว่าเด็กโต อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ
ACS standards: children <2 yr should receive anesthesia from pediatric specialty-trained anesthesiologist; ↓ anesthesia-related morbidity vs non-pediatric anesthesiologist
Retrospective analysis >350,000 surgical cases: risk factors for 30-day mortality:
Weight <5 kg; ASA status ≥III; preoperative sepsis; inotropic support; ventilator dependence prior to surgery
NECTARINE study (Europe): confirmed same comorbidities as primary risk determinants ใน neonatal surgical outcomes
1.2 Ethical Imperative

Anand et al Lancet 1987 (landmark): randomized trial preterm infants undergoing ligation PDA → nitrous oxide-only vs nitrous + fentanyl. Control group: massive ↑ epinephrine, norepinephrine, glucagon, cortisol → 17 periop complications vs 2 in fentanyl group

Neonates ARE capable of pain perception: no recallable memory before 3–4 yr (infantile amnesia), BUT neonates have consciousness + implicit memory — early pain → exaggerated later responses + impaired neurodevelopment
Goals of neonatal anesthesia: (1) block surgical stress response, (2) effective pain/discomfort management — both are clinical and ethical imperatives
1.3 Neurotoxicity — Animal vs Human Data

Anesthetic Neurotoxicity — ข้อเท็จจริงที่ต้องรู้
Animal studies: virtually ALL common anesthetics (volatile agents, propofol, ketamine, midazolam) → neuronal apoptosis + neurocognitive deficits in rats, primates (delayed learning, impaired memory, altered behavior). Translational uncertainty: dosing in animals often causes mortality; apoptosis-inductive doses ≠ clinical doses; surgery absent (removes surgical stress-stimulation balance). FDA 2016 warning: repeated/lengthy GA in children <3 yr or pregnant women. Human evidence (GAS, PANDA, MASK): single brief anesthetic in early life = safe — no neurodevelopmental difference at 2, 5 yr (GAS), 8–15 yr (PANDA, MASK). Multiple exposures: 3 large retrospective cohorts (>59,000 children combined) — insufficient evidence that age/multiple exposures cause adverse neurodevelopment. Bottom line: benefit of adequate anesthesia for surgery overwhelmingly outweighs theoretical risk; short single anesthetic = safe; delay surgery only when benefit >risk.
2. Preoperative Evaluation
2.1 Risk Assessment
ประเมิน GA, birth weight, postnatal age, current medical status, required ventilatory/hemodynamic support
Family history: malignant hyperthermia (MH) + pseudocholinesterase deficiency — MUST obtain; alter anesthetic drug choice completely
Maternal history: diabetes (↑ LGA, hypoglycemia risk); SLE (congenital heart block risk); preeclampsia; IUGR; congenital infections; drug/alcohol use
Medications timing: last feed (NPO compliance); vasoactive drips; ventilator settings (esp. if on HFOV → need switch to conventional + observe hours before transport)
2.2 NPO Guidelines
Clear fluids UP TO 2h before induction is ENCOURAGED: ↓ glucose fluctuations, ↓ hunger distress, ↑ parental satisfaction
Dehydration + hypoglycemia occur much faster in neonates than older patients — especially infant without IV line; prolonged NPO = risk
Full stomach cases (pyloric stenosis, duodenal atresia, malrotation): already NPO but significant gastric contents remain → rapid-sequence induction required; NGT drainage unreliable
2.3 Preoperative Lab and Imaging
Hemoglobin >10 g/dL required: fetal Hgb P50 = 20 mmHg (vs HgbA P50 = 27) → ↓ O2 unloading at tissue level; anemia → ↑ tissue hypoxia risk at surgery; anemia also ↑ postoperative apnea risk
Correct metabolic acidosis before surgery (fix the cause); correct hypocalcemia
Major procedure: have blood for transfusion available; FFP + platelets for major surgery

Congenital anomaly syndromes: review Table 39.3 (Anesthetic Implications of Neonatal Syndromes): Down, Pierre Robin, VACTERL, DiGeorge, Goldenhar, dwarfism, osteogenesis imperfecta — each has specific airway/cardiac/positioning implications
BPD patients: consider echocardiogram preop to assess pulmonary hypertension severity + right heart function
3. Neonatal Physiology and Anesthetic Concerns
3.1 Transitional Circulation and PPHN Risk

PPHN Perioperatively — Triggers + Management
Pulmonary arterioles retain abundant smooth muscle after birth → hyperreactive PVR. Triggers intraoperatively: (1) hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis → ↑ PVR → R→L shunt; (2) inhalational agents → ↓ SVR → R→L shunt; (3) surgical stress → catecholamine release → ↑ PVR; (4) anesthetic overdose → ↓ SVR. PPHN crisis: shunt fraction 70–80% → profound cyanosis. Monitoring: PRE- and POST-ductal SpO2 simultaneously — pre-ductal (right hand) vs post-ductal (either foot) → detect PDA shunt reversal early. Management: adequate analgesia/anesthesia (↓ catecholamine release); iNO + inotropic support immediately available for known/high-risk PPHN cases.
3.2 Respiratory Mechanics and Apnea
FRC ↓ relative to body size vs adults; alveolar ventilation/FRC ratio = 5:1 in neonates vs 1.5:1 in adults → rapid FiO2 changes → rapid SpO2 changes; desaturation very fast on airway loss
Closing volume ≈ FRC in neonates; anesthesia ↓ FRC → airway closure at end expiration → atelectasis, V/Q mismatch, ↑ intrapulmonary shunt
Laryngeal braking (auto-PEEP): awake infant maintains FRC via laryngeal braking → abolished by anesthesia → ↓ FRC further. PEEP/CPAP essential intraoperatively
Surfactant mature at 36 wk; immature alveoli (saccular) + very compliant chest wall → ↑ work of breathing
Resistance ∝ 1/r⁴ → small tube diameter + secretions → large resistance changes; endotracheal tube kinking + secretions = common
Controlled positive-pressure ventilation required for most neonates during surgery (low FRC + ↑ closing volume + anesthetic suppression of respiratory drive)
Tracheomalacia, bronchomalacia, laryngomalacia: common in premature → airway collapse with sedation or on expiration. CPAP/PEEP stents airway open
3.3 Postoperative Apnea

Postoperative Apnea — Cote Meta-analysis (Key Data)
Cote et al: meta-analysis of 8 separate studies. Risk inversely related to gestational age AND postconceptional age (PCA). Full-term neonate: risk small after 45 wk PCA. Preterm infant: at risk until 55–60 wk PCA. Additional risk factors: anemia, neurologic disease, current apneic episodes. Practice: neonates from home for elective surgery → admit for 12–24h respiratory monitoring — until >45 wk PCA (full term) OR >55–60 wk PCA (preterm). Conservative approach: avoid elective surgery until 60 wk PCA if possible. Central + obstructive + mixed apnea all occur.
Biphasic hypoxia response in premature: initial ↑ ventilation → followed by progressive ↓ → apnea. Anesthesia worsens this response significantly
3.4 Airway Anatomy — Neonatal Differences
Head large + prominent occiput + short neck: ↑ tendency for airway obstruction with sedation; optimal positioning: sniffing position with head on pad
Larynx at C4 (vs C5–C6 adult); tongue large; epiglottis soft and floppy; larynx elliptical not perfectly round in cross-section

Cuffed ET tubes now preferred even in neonates ≥3 kg: thin low-pressure cuffs (Microcuff) safe; prevent leaks when lung compliance changes; protect from aspiration; cuffed tube ↓ subglottic stenosis risk vs previously believed
For <3 kg: no cuffed ETT data (smallest cuffed = 3.0 mm ID) → uncuffed for smallest premies
1 mm edema at cricoid ring → 60% ↓ cross-sectional area → dramatic ↑ work of breathing; any laryngeal trauma must be taken seriously
LMA size 1 for neonates <5 kg: useful for Pierre Robin, Goldenhar — temporary ventilation bridge while preparing for definitive airway
3.5 Cardiac Physiology Under Anesthesia
Neonatal heart: operates at HIGH end of Starling curve (high EDV from left-to-right transition). CO depends almost entirely on heart rate (140–160/min) — cannot increase SV significantly
All inhalational agents → cardiovascular depression + ↓ contractility + ↓ autonomic tone + peripheral vasodilation; baroreceptor reflex blunted → unable to compensate for volume loss
Almost all neonates need some volume loading during anesthesia to support tissue perfusion; inotropic support in sick neonate
Single ventricle physiology: high FiO2 → pulmonary vasodilation → pulmonary overcirculation at expense of systemic → acidosis. Sometimes deliberate CO2 ↑ to ↑ PVR and limit pulmonary blood flow
PDA (most common L→R shunt): assess for CHF signs (hepatic congestion, enlarged heart, edema); may need inotropes during anesthesia
3.6 Fetal Hemoglobin and Anemia
~80% fetal Hgb (HgbF) at birth: P50 = 20 mmHg vs HgbA P50 = 27 mmHg → HgbF has higher O2 affinity → oxyhemoglobin curve shifted LEFT → ↓ O2 unloading at tissues
Compensated by high starting Hgb ~17.5 g/dL at birth; but during surgery: ↓ PaO2 or ↓ CO → ↑ tissue hypoxia risk with metabolic acidosis
Physiologic nadir 9.5–11 g/dL at 7–9 wk; worse + earlier in preterm (↓ EPO production, ↑ phlebotomy, ↓ RBC lifespan)
Preoperative transfusion target: Hgb ≥10 g/dL before noncardiac surgery — anemia = independent risk factor for postop mortality (Goobie et al); also ↑ apnea risk
3.7 Renal Physiology
GFR at birth: ~30% of adult GFR; nephrogenesis complete at 34 wk; ↑ rapidly over first year → adult levels by ~1 yr
Limited concentrating ability + obligate Na waster (immature proximal tubule): tendency toward ↓ Na, especially if free water given
Cannot handle large Na load either → volume overload → CHF; frequent fluid/blood product admin during surgery requires careful renal physiology awareness
Mild proximal RTA at birth: serum bicarb ~20 mmol/L; all worse in preterm (<34 wk)
3.8 Temperature Regulation

Thermoregulation — Perioperative Hypothermia Risk
Large SA, small mass, minimal insulation → extreme heat loss. Anesthesia blunts thermoregulatory sensitivity + ↓ nonshivering thermogenesis (NST) via brown fat inhibition + ↑ cutaneous vasodilation. Preterm: less brown fat → ↓ NST capacity. Cold stress → catecholamine release → ↑ PVR → ↑ cardiac stress → ↑ O2 consumption. Hypothermia consequences: ↑ metabolic O2 demand, respiratory depression, bradycardia, coagulopathy, impaired wound healing, prolonged neuromuscular blockade. Prevention: neutral thermal environment always (incubator for transport); forced-air warmer; warm fluids + blood products; overhead radiant warmer; hats; ↑ OR temperature; core temp monitoring (rectal/esophageal — NOT skin).
3.9 Carbohydrate Metabolism
Whole-body glucose demand (per kg) ~2x adults; hepatic glycogen depleted rapidly, especially in LBW infants
Symptoms of hypoglycemia MASKED under anesthesia: jitteriness, altered LOC, apnea, tachypnea — monitor serum glucose for procedures >2–3h
Even transient hypoglycemia → neurologic injury + developmental delay in neonates
Maintain IV glucose during NPO + intraoperatively; use 2.5% dextrose intraoperatively (↓ hyperglycemia risk vs D10); surgical stress → ↑ catecholamines → hyperglycemia
Preterm: insulin response deficient + high catecholamines → hyperglycemia during surgery; need glucose monitoring from both sides
4. Transport to the Operating Room
4.1 Transport Risks
Transport itself may be as dangerous as the surgery in premature neonates — especially cold stress, tube dislodgement, monitor failures
Use dedicated transport incubator with built-in ventilator, air/O2 supplies, blender for unstable infants
Heat loss during transport: incubator (unplugged) retains heat; radiant warmer baby → cover including head; plastic wrap + chemical warmers for extreme preterm

Hand-bagging during transport: CPAP/PEEP generally lost; O2 tank limits FiO2 to 100%; add air tank + blender for controlled FiO2; ALWAYS include manometer with anesthesia bag — easy to reach barotrauma-causing pressures
Endotracheal tube: carry laryngoscope + ETT + face mask on every transport; small changes in tube position = extubation or endobronchial in micro-premies
Noninvasive ventilation (NCPAP/NIPPV): not easily portable → most require intubation for OR; plan respiratory mode transition before transport
Physician experienced in neonatal resuscitation should accompany; monitoring minimum: pulse oximetry; ECG + BP if arterial line present
4.2 Bedside Surgery in NICU
For appropriate surgical procedures: prefer bedside NICU surgery — avoids transport risk for most critically ill infants
Requirements: clean closed area, adequate lights + instruments, monitors, anesthesia equipment; no anesthesia machine needed in premies — standard neonatal ventilator usable
State-of-the-art NICUs: dedicated OR within NICU for extreme cases
5. Inhalational Anesthetic Agents
5.1 Uptake and Pharmacokinetics in Neonates

Inhalational agents reach brain FASTER in neonates than adults: (1) alveolar ventilation/FRC = 5:1 (vs 1.5:1 adult) → alveolar [anesthetic] equilibrates rapidly with inspired; (2) greater fraction cardiac output to vessel-rich group (brain); (3) lower blood solubility in neonates → faster uptake
EASY TO OVERDOSE with inhalational agents in neonates — especially preterm. Close monitoring essential
5.2 MAC and Postconceptional Age

MAC and Postconceptional Age — Isoflurane Reference
MAC (minimum alveolar concentration): concentration at which 50% patients show no response to surgical incision. MAC inversely related to prematurity: lowest in very preterm; ↑ to peak at PCA ~1 yr; then ↓ through adulthood. Isoflurane MAC: <32 wk GA: ~1.28%; 32–37 wk: ~1.41%; term neonate: ~1.60%; 1–6 months: ~1.87% (LeDez Anesthesiology 1987). Implication: preterm neonate needs LESS agent for anesthesia than term → lower MAC → ↑ risk of overdose with same concentration. MAC is potency metric — used to guide dosing adjustment.
5.3 Individual Agents

Sevoflurane: NON-irritating to airway → useful for inhalation induction; almost insoluble in blood → very fast onset; cardiovascular effects similar to isoflurane; associated with QTc prolongation in adults + infants — monitor ECG

Isoflurane: very pungent → CANNOT use for mask induction (laryngospasm); ↓ SVR → hypotension (↓ preload worsens); ↑ cerebral blood flow without ↑ ICP (cerebral protective via ↓ metabolism). Moderate volume loading before induction
Desflurane: pungent; rarely used in neonates; faster emergence

Nitrous oxide: MAC 104% → cannot achieve alone at normal pressure; almost insoluble → fast onset. EXPANDS GAS-FILLED SPACES: bowel distention (surgical problem), expands pneumothorax; if ventilation interrupted → rapidly fills alveoli → hypoxic mixture → rapid desaturation. Limited use in neonates: adjunct during mask induction only; switch to air-O2 during maintenance
6. Intravenous Anesthetic Agents
6.1 Induction Agents

Propofol: phenol derivative in lipid emulsion; fast redistribution + hepatic metabolism → rapid recovery. Approved >2 months; significant interindividual variability in neonates. ↓ clearance (immature liver) + ↓ fat/muscle for redistribution → prolonged effect in premies. Large initial dose needed (2–3 mg/kg: large extracellular volume). Potent vasodilator → significant hypotension after bolus — limits use in preterm

Ketamine: NMDA receptor antagonist; dissociative anesthesia with excellent analgesia + hemodynamic stability. Sympathomimetic: ↑ BP, HR, ↑ SVR → useful in cardiovascular-compromised infants; useful for breaking hypercyanotic spells in Tetralogy of Fallot (↑ SVR → ↓ R→L shunt). Caution: ↑ ICP (contraindicated if hydrocephalus/IVH risk); secretions ↑ (glycopyrrolate pre-treatment)
6.2 Benzodiazepines

Midazolam: short-acting; sedation + anxiolysis + amnesia; NO significant analgesia. Hepatic metabolism prolonged in preterm. Cochrane meta-analysis (3 studies): all showed effective sedation BUT one study: ↑ poor neurologic outcome or death in midazolam group + ↑ LOS — raises safety questions for infusion in neonates
Midazolam bolus + opioid: ↑ risk respiratory depression — use cautiously
Lorazepam: longer-acting benzodiazepine; used for NICU sedation; less studied for anesthesia
6.3 Opioids

Fentanyl — Standard Opioid สำหรับ Neonatal Anesthesia
Fentanyl: most commonly used opioid in premature neonates; wide safety margin; blocks pulmonary hypertensive crises → useful in CDH, PPHN, some cardiac defects. Pharmacokinetics highly variable: clearance lowest in most premature; ↑ with GA + postnatal age (hepatic maturation); ↑ intra-abdominal pressure → ↓ hepatic blood flow → ↓ clearance. Start low: 0.25 mcg/kg, titrate to effect; wide therapeutic range even at high doses → hemodynamic stability maintained. High dose fentanyl + muscle relaxant = standard for very premature neonate anesthesia; add benzodiazepine or volatile agent to prevent awareness. ALL opioids: respiratory depressants → prolonged respiratory depression with higher doses → may need postop ventilation.
Morphine: standard analgesic (longer duration); histamine release limits high-dose use as sole anesthetic; ↑ duration in preterm (immature hepatic metabolism) → less frequent dosing needed

Remifentanil: terminated by tissue esterase hydrolysis → NO accumulation; half-life 4.4 min, clearance 80 mL/kg/min in neonates. Short duration → useful for procedural analgesia; infusion → opioid-induced tolerance + hyperalgesia

Acetaminophen: COX inhibitor → ↓ prostaglandin synthesis; IV formulation now allows intraoperative use. After major abdominal/thoracic: ↓ opioid consumption. Dose adjustment by PCA: 7.5–10 mg/kg for <36 wk PCA (↓ clearance → hepatic toxicity risk with standard dosing)

Dexmedetomidine: alpha-2 agonist; hypnotic + analgesic + anxiolytic; minimal effect on respiratory function → promotes early extubation; proven effective for post-cardiac surgery sedation. Adverse: bradycardia, hypotension, paradoxical hypertension. ↓ clearance in preterm (immature hepatocytes). Animal data: may protect against neuroapoptosis from other anesthetics — promising but needs further human study
7. Neuromuscular Blocking Agents
7.1 Depolarizing Agent
Succinylcholine: only depolarizing agent; rapid onset + rapid offset; ED95 in neonates: 2–3 mg/kg (vs 0.5–1 mg/kg adult) (neonates require MORE per kg). Metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase.

Contraindications/risks: (1) myopathies/neurologic disease → life-threatening hyperkalemia + cardiac arrest refractory to resuscitation; (2) bradycardia during intubation → some give atropine prophylactically. Reserved primarily for rapid-sequence induction (full stomach) in pediatrics
7.2 Nondepolarizing Agents

rows: May cause bradycardia with narcotics; prolonged in preterm
Duration NOT affected by liver/kidney — safe in organ dysfunction
Rapid onset; can replace succinylcholine for RSI
Duration dose-dependent + MUCH longer than succinylcholine; prolonged in preterm

blocks.text: Monitoring: train-of-four (TOF) nerve stimulator — challenging in small premature; hypothermia prolongs blockade → ↓ hypothermia before reversing/extubating

Reversal: neostigmine (anticholinesterase) + glycopyrrolate or atropine (anticholinergic); sugammadex: reverses rocuronium/vecuronium (even profound blockade); approved >2 yr but case series show safe off-label use in neonates; cannot be used with neostigmine for anything except vecuronium/rocuronium
8. Induction and Maintenance of General Anesthesia
8.1 Standard IV Induction
Most sick neonates: IV induction with induction agent (propofol or ketamine) + fentanyl + neuromuscular blocker
Glycopyrrolate or atropine before laryngoscopy: optional — mitigates vagal response/bradycardia
Mask induction (healthier neonates without IV): sevoflurane in 50% N2O; obtain IV rapidly after induction
8.2 Rapid-Sequence Induction (RSI)
Indication: full stomach (bowel obstruction, malrotation, pyloric stenosis) → ↑ aspiration risk with standard induction

Steps: (1) preoxygenation with 100% FiO2; (2) IV induction agent (propofol) + fast-acting relaxant (succinylcholine OR rocuronium) in rapid succession; (3) NO cricoid pressure routinely — can hinder glottic view (Sellick maneuver no longer routine in infants); (4) gentle positive-pressure ventilation avoided; (5) intubate after 45–90 sec. RSI requires significant expertise — errors not tolerated
8.3 Difficult Airway Management
Difficult airway syndromes: Pierre Robin, Goldenhar, CHARGE, Treacher Collins, micrognathia syndromes → plan ahead
Maintain spontaneous ventilation if significant concern for difficult airway (Pierre Robin, Goldenhar); secure airway BEFORE paralysis
Awake intubation now RARELY used: ↑ airway trauma + hemodynamic consequences (hypertension, PPHN crisis, ↑ ICP) — abandoned in modern practice
Tools: pediatric fiberoptic bronchoscope (passes through 2.5 ETT; no suction channel); video laryngoscope; Miller blade 00/0/1; LMA size 1 as bridge → bronchoscope through LMA → ETT over scope
ENT consultation for rigid bronchoscopy in extreme cases
8.4 Maintenance
Full monitoring: continuous ECG, SpO2, end-tidal CO2, blood pressure, temperature, FiO2, peak IP, tidal volume, PEEP

NIRS (near-infrared spectroscopy): provides real-time cerebral tissue oxygenation; routinely used in pediatric cardiac surgery + PICU/NICU; insufficient data yet to confirm benefit on neurodevelopmental outcome under anesthesia
Fluids via infusion pump only (never pressure bag in small neonates): ↓ inadvertent flush; 0.5 mL/h via syringe pump vs 3 mL/h via pressure bag through transducer
Third-space losses: 2–15 mL/kg/h during open bowel procedures (gastroschisis, omphalocele, bowel resection); replace with balanced crystalloid (LR); albumin 5% for hypoalbuminemic
Blood products: packed RBCs (washed, leukoreduced, irradiated); FFP + platelets for major procedures — prepare before start
End-tidal CO2 in neonates: waveform capnography → qualitative confirmation of intubation; quantitative accuracy limited by dead space + high RR + short expiration times → correlate with ABG
8.5 Recovery and Extubation
Smallest/sickest preterm: NOT extubated at end of surgery — return to NICU for continued ventilation; immaturity ↓ drug clearance → prolonged anesthetic effect

Criteria for extubation: regular rhythmic respiration (may need ↓ ventilation toward end to allow mild hypercapnia → restore respiratory drive); adequate volume status; Hgb near-normal (anemia prolongs recovery + ↑ apnea); neuromuscular blockade fully reversed; normothermic; flexion of hip + rectus contraction = adequate motor strength
Laryngospasm risk at extubation: anesthesia team must be ready to mask ventilate + reintubate immediately
Monitor postop apnea: admit for 12–24h monitoring after last apnea episode if former preterm; conservative approach: avoid elective surgery until 60 wk PCA
9. Regional Anesthesia in the Neonate
9.1 Rationale and Safety
Increasing interest in regional anesthesia: concerns about GA neurotoxicity + improved ultrasound guidance → safer, easier blocks

PRAN (Pediatric Regional Anesthesia Network): >100,000 blocks in children — major complications exceedingly rare; NO permanent neurologic deficits reported; includes ~10,000 blocks in neonates/infants
Regional most commonly combined with GA: ↓ intraoperative anesthetic requirements, ↓ postoperative analgesic needs, promote earlier extubation

Sole anesthetic (no GA): used in high-risk neonates (severe BPD) where intubation avoidance preferred; GAS trial: spinal anesthesia ↓ clinically significant apneas vs GA (but no overall reduction in postop apnea)
9.2 Neuraxial Techniques

Caudal anesthesia: single-shot through sacrococcygeal membrane (22G needle or angiocath) → epidural space; useful for lower abdomen, groin, lower extremity surgery. Bupivacaine or ropivacaine → pain-free ≥4h. Often combined with GA for ↓ volatile agent dose + postop analgesia

Epidural catheter: placed via caudal route or directly at thoracic/lumbar level → continuous infusion for multi-day analgesia; no hemodynamic compromise; PRAN neuraxial catheter study (>300 neonates <1 mo): most common complications = catheter malfunction, contamination, vascular aspiration — NO persistent neurologic injury, deep infection, or epidural hematoma

Spinal anesthesia: 25G Quincke needle at L4–L5 (below cauda equina); tetracaine or bupivacaine → dense block T5–T6 dermatome; for inguinal hernia repair. Duration <90 min; clonidine adjunct can extend to ~100 min
9.3 Fascial Plane Blocks

Truncal/Fascial Plane Blocks — Comparison Table
Quadratus lumborum (QL) block: ipsilateral T7/T8–T12/L1; abdominal + upper pelvic surgery; risk: kidney puncture, intraperitoneal injection. Serratus anterior block: ipsilateral T2–T6; thoracic surgery; risk: pneumothorax, IV injection. Erector spinae plane (ESP) block: 3–4 dermatome levels from injection point; thoracic + upper abdominal surgery; risk: pneumothorax. Advantages vs neuraxial: less invasive; local anesthetic deposited in fascial plane (not near neuraxial space/large nerves); relatively easy in neonates under US guidance; gaining rapid popularity.
9.4 Local Anesthetic Toxicity (LAST)
Neonates at ↑ LAST risk: immature hepatic enzymes → ↓ clearance → accumulation with continuous infusions

Maximum doses reduced in neonates vs adults; bupivacaine + ropivacaine = amide class → hepatic metabolism. 2-Chloroprocaine: ester class → plasma cholinesterase hydrolysis → very short half-life → preferred for continuous epidural infusions to ↓ LAST risk

Local infiltration/nerve block: circumcision → dorsal penile nerve block (DPNB) or penile ring block effective as sole anesthetic; EMLA cream: venipuncture, LP, minor procedures (allow 45–60 min contact time)
10. OR Equipment and Monitoring Standards
10.1 Environment
OR should surpass NICU capabilities — neonate has additional stress of surgery on top of existing illness

Temperature control: radiant lamps before draping; heating pad on OR table; forced-air heater (full-access blanket under infant); dry sheets (wet = evaporative cooling); warm fluids/blood; measure core temp (rectal/esophageal/nasopharyngeal — NOT skin)
10.2 Monitoring Requirements

rows: Peak IP, tidal volume, PEEP, FiO2
Invasive arterial line (radial or umbilical artery)

blocks.text: Arterial line tips: use small syringe (no suction on artery — spasm risk); flush gently; infusion pump at 0.5 mL/h (NOT pressure bag at 3 mL/h) for small neonates

Ventilator choice: modern anesthesia machines with sophisticated ventilators (volume-limited or pressure-limited; measure expired TV ≥10 mL; PEEP without external valves) — usable at ≥1000 g; for <1500 g: NICU ventilator may be more accurate but prevents volatile agents → TIVA required
11. NICU Practical — Post-Anesthesia Care ใน NICU
11.1 On Return from OR — Standard Handoff Items

Receive formal anesthesia handoff: agents used (volatile? IV? opioid dose/timing?); neuromuscular blocker given + reversal status; fluid balance + blood products; glucose management; temperature on exit
Fentanyl residual: dose, timing of last bolus → predict respiratory depression duration; plan for assisted ventilation if large intraop dose given; monitor SpO2 and apnea monitor mandatory
Glucose: resume glucose infusion immediately post-op; check serum glucose within 30 min of return; surgical stress may cause transient hyperglycemia then rebound hypoglycemia
Temperature: hypothermia = common after OR → prolongs neuromuscular blockade + drug metabolism + coagulopathy; warm actively on return; target 36.5–37.5°C
11.2 Disease-Specific Post-Op Care

BPD post-op: expect ↑ FiO2 needs (atelectasis, residual anesthetic effect); airway hyperreactivity → bronchodilators; right heart function: echocardiogram if hemodynamic instability; careful fluid balance; electrolytes if on diuretics
CDH/PPHN post-op: maintain iNO; continue pulmonary vasodilators; monitor pre- and postductal SpO2 at all times; avoid hypoventilation (↓ pH → ↑ PVR) and hyperoxia

Cardiac post-op (single ventricle): FiO2 balance critical — avoid high O2 (pulmonary overcirculation); target SpO2 70–85% depending on physiology; discuss FiO2 target with cardiac surgery + cardiology
Post-RSI (bowel obstruction/malrotation): residual aspiration risk; watch for pneumonitis; Replogle suction; NG decompression; NPO; serial CXR
Post-op apnea monitoring: standard apnea/bradycardia monitor for ALL post-op neonates; preterm <55–60 wk PCA → minimum 12–24h apnea-free monitoring before discharge
11.3 Pain Assessment and Management
Use validated pain scales: NIPS (Neonatal Infant Pain Scale), CRIES, N-PASS for intubated premies, PIPP
Non-pharmacologic: non-nutritive sucking, oral sucrose (24%, 0.5 mL at 2 min before procedure — lasts ~5–7 min), facilitated tucking, kangaroo care when possible

Systemic analgesia post-op: IV morphine for significant pain (major surgery); acetaminophen as adjunct (IV 7.5–10 mg/kg q6h for <36 wk PCA, q6h for ≥36 wk at 10–15 mg/kg); avoid NSAIDs (renal function concern in neonates); fentanyl for hemodynamic instability
12. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Postoperative Apnea: 55–60 wk PCA Rule
Cote meta-analysis (8 studies): apnea risk inversely related to GA + PCA. Full-term: risk small after 45 wk PCA. Preterm: at risk until 55–60 wk PCA. Anemia + neurologic disease + ongoing apnea = additional risk factors. ทุก preterm ที่ return from OR จาก surgery = apnea monitor ≥12–24h หลัง last apnea. Elective surgery: delay until 60 wk PCA ถ้าทำได้. รู้ PCA ของทารกก่อนรับกลับ — check surgeon note สำหรับ gestational age at birth + current age → calculate PCA.

PEARL 2 — Fentanyl ไม่ใช่ Complete Anesthetic: Add Benzodiazepine หรือ Volatile
High-dose fentanyl ให้ analgesia ดีเยี่ยม แต่ไม่ใช่ complete anesthetic — awareness ยังเกิดได้ใน adults. ปัจจุบัน: benzodiazepine (midazolam) หรือ inhalational agent (isoflurane) ถูก add เสมอใน OR. เมื่อ fentanyl กลับจาก OR: คาดว่าทารกจะ sedated + respiratory depressed ตาม dose + timing; ถ้าทารก unexpectedly alert + tachycardic post-op — ไม่ใช่แปลว่า pain เสมอ — อาจมี undersedation ระหว่าง surgery (awareness). Communicate กับ anesthesia team ทันที.

PEARL 3 — Hypothermia ป้องกันไม่ได้ถ้าไม่รีบ Warm ทันทีที่กลับจาก OR
OR มักเย็น; anesthesia ↓ thermoregulation + ↓ NST; open body cavity = massive heat loss. ผลของ hypothermia: prolonged neuromuscular blockade (ยาง rocuronium/vecuronium อยู่นานขึ้น) → apnea; coagulopathy → bleeding; bradycardia + ↑ PVR → hemodynamic collapse. เมื่อรับทารกกลับ NICU: (1) ตรวจ rectal temp ทันที; (2) radiant warmer + servo mode; (3) ถ้า <36°C → active warming ด้วย warm mattress + warm air; (4) ห้าม extubate ถ้า hypothermic — neuromuscular block ยังไม่หาย fully.

PEARL 4 — MAC ↓ ใน Preterm: ยาน้อยลงแต่ Risk ↑
MAC ของ isoflurane: <32 wk = 1.28% vs 1–6 mo = 1.87% (LeDez 1987). Preterm ต้องการ inhalational agent น้อยกว่า term — แต่ uptake เร็วกว่ามาก (alveolar ventilation/FRC ratio ↑). ผลที่ตามมา: ถ้าใช้ concentration เดิมกับทารก term ใน preterm → overdose → bradycardia + ↓ CO rapidly. เมื่อ anesthesia ผิดปกติ (bradycardia ทันทีหลัง induction) — think anesthetic overdose ใน preterm ก่อน. Dexmedetomidine: อาจ neuroprotective ต่อ neuroapoptosis จาก agents อื่น — ยังอยู่ใน study stage.

PEARL 5 — PPHN Crisis ใน OR: Pre/Post-ductal SpO2 = Early Warning System
PPHN triggers ใน OR: hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, inadequate analgesia (catecholamine release), ↓ SVR จาก inhalational agents → R→L shunt flip. Prevention: (1) monitor pre-ductal SpO2 (right hand) + post-ductal SpO2 (foot) simultaneously — gap ≥5–10% = ductal shunting; (2) adequate anesthesia/analgesia reduces catecholamine surge; (3) iNO + inotrope support immediately available for high-risk PPHN cases. เมื่อทารก PPHN กลับจาก OR: expect ↑ iNO requirements post-op (surgical stress + catecholamines ลดลงช้า); ห้าม extubate ทันทีหลัง OR ถ้า PPHN ไม่ stable — risk acute PPHN crisis at extubation.
13. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Anand KJ, Sippell WG, Aynsley-Green A Lancet 1987;1:243: RCT preterm infants PDA ligation; nitrous oxide-only vs nitrous + fentanyl; fentanyl group: no ↑ epinephrine/norepinephrine; 17 periop complications in control vs 2 in fentanyl — established ethical imperative for adequate analgesia

Davidson AJ et al (GAS Trial) Lancet 2016;387:239: RCT sevoflurane GA vs awake regional anesthesia (spinal/caudal) for herniorrhaphy in infancy; no difference in neurodevelopmental outcomes at 2-yr follow-up; 5-yr follow-up confirmed (Lancet 2019) — single brief anesthetic safe

Sun LS et al (PANDA Study) JAMA 2016;315:2312: prospective sibling-matched cohort; herniorrhaphy in infants vs unexposed sibling; neuropsychological assessment age 8–15 yr — no difference in full-scale IQ or neurocognitive outcomes
Warner DO et al (MASK Study) Anesthesiology 2018;129:89: matched cohort ~1000 children; neuropsychological testing age 8–12 or 15–19 yr — GA in childhood NOT associated with deficits in cognition
FDA Drug Safety Communication 2016: warning against repeated or lengthy use of GA + sedation drugs in children <3 yr and pregnant women — risk of abnormal brain development

Cote CJ et al Anesthesiology 1995;82:809: meta-analysis 8 studies postoperative apnea in preterm infants; risk inversely related to gestational age + PCA; full-term: safe after 45 wk PCA; preterm: risk until 55–60 wk PCA; anemia = additional risk factor
LeDez KM Anesthesiology 1987;67:301: isoflurane MAC vs postconceptional age (semilog scale); MAC: <32 wk: 1.28%, 32–37 wk: 1.41%, term neonate: 1.60%, 1–6 mo: 1.87% — inversely related to prematurity
Walker BJ et al (PRAN) Anesth Analg 2018;127:1042: >100,000 regional anesthetics in children; major complications exceedingly rare; NO permanent neurologic deficits; includes ~10,000 neonates/infants

Lönnqvist PA et al (PRAN neuraxial catheters) Reg Anesth Pain Med 2021;46:475: >300 neuraxial catheters in infants <1 mo; common complications: malfunction, contamination, vascular aspiration; NO persistent neurologic injury, no deep infection, no epidural hematoma

Heydinger G, Tobias J, Veneziano G Anaesthesia 2021;76:74: fundamentals + innovations in regional anesthesia for infants and children; QL, serratus anterior, ESP block comparisons; ultrasound guidance transforming neonatal regional practice
Goobie SM et al Anesthesiology 2018;128:916: preoperative anemia = independent risk factor for 30-day mortality after noncardiac surgery in infants; Hgb >10 g/dL target pre-op

Bhatt DR et al Pediatr Anesth 2012;22: retrospective analysis >350,000 pediatric surgical cases; weight <5 kg, ASA ≥III, sepsis, inotropic support, ventilator dependence = top 5 risk factors for 30-day mortality — validated perioperative risk stratification

NECTARINE Study Group Br J Anaesth 2021;126:1173: European multicenter observational study; same comorbidities (weight, ASA, sepsis, inotropes, ventilation) confirmed as primary determinants of perioperative mortality/morbidity in neonates

Disma N et al (GAS trial 5-yr) Lancet Child Adolesc Health 2019;3:498: sevoflurane vs awake-regional herniorrhaphy; 5-yr follow-up Bayley III cognitive composite scores: no difference — longest prospective evidence for single brief anesthetic safety

Tobias JD Curr Opin Anaesthesiol 2014;27:303: dexmedetomidine in pediatric anesthesia: effective analgesic/sedative after cardiac surgery; ↓ narcotic requirements; minimal respiratory depression; animal data suggest neuroprotective against anesthetic-induced apoptosis
14. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ทำไม risk ของ postoperative apnea ใน premature infant ถึงยังสูงอยู่แม้ปิดยา GA แล้ว?

ตอบ: Multiple mechanisms: (1) immature respiratory control center (central apnea) — แย่ลงจาก anesthetic depression of central CO2 response; (2) floppy airway (obstructive apnea) — laryngo/tracheomalacia; (3) ↓ drug clearance in preterm → prolonged opioid/benzodiazepine residual effect; (4) anemia → ↑ apnea risk; (5) biphasic hypoxia response: initial ↑ ventilation then progressive ↓. Cote meta-analysis: risk inversely ∝ GA + PCA → preterm at risk until 55–60 wk PCA.
Q: Intraoperative PPHN crisis: ตรวจพบอย่างไร และ immediate management คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Detection: SpO2 gap pre-ductal (right hand) minus post-ductal (foot) ≥5–10% = ductal shunting (R→L); ↓ SpO2 post-ductal + maintained pre-ductal. Management: (1) ↑ FiO2 transiently; (2) ↑ analgesia/sedation (↓ catecholamine trigger); (3) correct hypercapnia + acidosis; (4) initiate or ↑ iNO dose; (5) start/↑ inotropes for hemodynamic support; (6) notify surgeon to pause if possible. Prevention: adequate anesthesia depth + iNO immediately available for high-risk cases.
Q: ทำไม MAC ของ isoflurane ถึงต่ำกว่าใน preterm infant มากกว่า term neonate? มีผลทางคลินิกอย่างไร?

ตอบ: MAC ∝ CNS maturity/myelination — immature CNS = more sensitive to anesthetic agents. MAC inversely ∝ prematurity: <32 wk = 1.28% vs term = 1.60% vs 1–6 mo = 1.87% (LeDez 1987). Clinical implication: preterm requires LOWER concentration for adequate anesthesia BUT uptake speed is also FASTER (alveolar ventilation/FRC ratio 5:1 + lower blood solubility) → overdose occurs rapidly and unexpectedly if same concentration used as term neonate → bradycardia, ↓ CO. Titrate carefully; use MAC as reference and go LOW in premies.
Q: Succinylcholine ใน neonate: ED95 เท่าไหร่ และ contraindication ที่สำคัญมีอะไร?

ตอบ: ED95 in neonates: 2–3 mg/kg (vs ~0.5–1 mg/kg in adults) — neonates need 3–5x more per kg; metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase (family history of pseudocholinesterase deficiency = must screen!). Contraindications: (1) myopathies/occult myopathies/neurologic disease → lethal hyperkalemia + cardiac arrest refractory to resuscitation; (2) malignant hyperthermia susceptibility (MHS) — family history essential; (3) Reserved for RSI (full stomach) in pediatrics; NOT routine use in neonates. Atropine often co-administered to prevent bradycardia during intubation.
Q: NPO guidelines ใน neonate: ทำไม clear fluids ถึง allow ได้ถึง 2 ชั่วโมงก่อนยา?

ตอบ: Clear fluids are rapidly absorbed; gastric emptying of clear liquids is fast (essentially complete by 2h). Benefits of allowing clear fluids up to 2h: (1) ↓ blood glucose fluctuations during NPO; (2) ↓ dehydration risk (neonates dehydrate much faster than older patients); (3) ↓ neonatal distress from hunger; (4) ↑ parental satisfaction. ข้อควรระวัง: full stomach cases (bowel obstruction) → cannot rely on NPO alone — significant gastric contents remain even after NPO → RSI required. NGT drainage unreliable and inadequate.
Q: Caudal anesthesia ใน neonate: ข้อดีเหนือ GA คืออะไร และ technique คร่าวๆ เป็นอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Benefits: ↓ intraoperative volatile agent requirements; excellent postoperative analgesia ≥4h; ↓ postop opioid use; may allow extubation sooner; ↓ anesthetic neurotoxicity exposure. In high-risk neonates (severe BPD): can be used as sole anesthetic to avoid intubation entirely. Technique: 22G short-bevel needle or angiocath through sacrococcygeal membrane into caudal epidural space; bupivacaine or ropivacaine (higher dose than spinal → watch LAST); single injection for 4h coverage; catheter can be threaded to thoracic level for extended analgesia.
Q: Dexmedetomidine ใน neonatal anesthesia: mechanism, benefits, และข้อควรระวัง?

ตอบ: Mechanism: highly specific alpha-2 adrenergic agonist → hypnotic + analgesic + anxiolytic effects via ↓ norepinephrine release from locus ceruleus (CNS sedation) + peripheral vasoconstriction. Key benefit: minimal respiratory depression → ↓ need for analgesics causing respiratory depression → promotes earlier extubation. Proven effective for post-cardiac surgery sedation in neonates. Animal data: neuroprotective against anesthetic neuroapoptosis — promising. Adverse effects: bradycardia, hypotension, paradoxical hypertension. ↓ clearance in preterm (immature hepatocytes) → prolonged effect → start low + monitor.
Q: PANDA study พิสูจน์อะไรเกี่ยวกับ neurotoxicity ของ GA ในทารก?

ตอบ: PANDA (Pediatric Anesthesia and Neurodevelopment Assessment) JAMA 2016: prospective sibling-matched cohort — compared neurocognitive outcomes of infants who received single, short inhaled GA for herniorrhaphy vs their biological sibling with NO anesthetic exposure. Follow-up at age 8–15 yr: NO difference in full-scale IQ, specific cognitive domains, behavior, or neuropsychological outcomes. Study strength: sibling controls minimize genetic/environmental confounders. Conclusion: single brief early anesthetic appears safe for neurodevelopment — consistent with GAS trial + MASK study.
Q: ทารกที่มี malignant hyperthermia susceptibility (MHS): ต้องเตรียม anesthesia อย่างไร?

ตอบ: MHS: autosomal dominant mutation in RYR1 (ryanodine receptor) or DHPR gene → uncontrolled calcium release from SR → hyperthermia, rigidity, acidosis, myoglobinuria, cardiac arrest. Trigger agents: all volatile agents (sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane) + succinylcholine — ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED. Safe anesthetic: TIVA (propofol + opioid + nondepolarizing relaxant only); use clean machine (purge anesthesia machine; remove vaporizers; flush with high-flow air ≥10 min). Treatment if MH crisis: dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV STAT (rapidly; repeat PRN); active cooling; correct acidosis + hyperkalemia. Screening: family history essential — obtain before EVERY anesthetic.

Day 137 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.40
บทต่อไป: Chapter 40 — Care of Periviable Micropreemies: The Japanese Perspective. เตรียมตัว: periviability definition (22–25 wk), Japanese NICU outcomes data for extreme preterm; philosophical + ethical frameworks for active resuscitation at periviability; survival rates + neurodevelopmental outcomes by gestational week; comparison Japan vs US vs European approaches; resuscitation decision frameworks; counseling parents before periviable birth; intensive care thresholds and shared decision-making.
